data.title: The baby-faced union boss making commuters miserable
data.description:
He’s a baby-faced, softly spoken former market trader from Deptford, south-east London, with a penchant for bingo.

Yet for all his disarming traits, Eddie Dempsey is causing misery for millions of workers and tourists in London this week as he leads crippling strikes on the London Underground.

Dempsey, 43, is the head of the National Union of Rail, Maritime and Transport Workers (RMT) and cut from the same cloth as his chip-on-the-shoulder predecessors Mick Lynch and Bob Crow.

While other union bosses often toed the line and kowtowed to the Left, Dempsey has displayed an alarming capacity to push the envelope – even going so far as to praise a noted Russian warlord after Putin’s initial invasion of Ukraine.

During a visit to the country in 2015 following Russia’s incursion into the Donbas region, he was pictured posing with and praising pro-Putin militia leaders.

Dempsey had gone into Ukraine on a “humanitarian” mission but was pictured alongside Aleksey Mozgovoy, the former head of the pro-Russian “Ghost Brigade”.

Mozgovoy, who had sought to ban young women from bars in the territories he controlled while urging them to have children to boost the Russian-speaking population, was killed two weeks after the visit.

Later, the union leader wrote a tribute describing him as a “charismatic” insurgent.

Dempsey, who was elected to the RMT leadership in March, has also previously clashed with the Left over his support for Brexit and the white working classes. He suggested that backers of Tommy Robinson, regardless of their bigotry, were “right to hate” the liberal Left.

He added that some in Labour had calculated that support from more affluent workers, together with ethnic minorities and liberals, would be enough to get into power and that the interests of the majority of workers could be sacrificed.

On Wednesday, with commuters hit by the misery of the Tube strike, Dempsey again chose to provoke the Left, lashing out at Labour and attacking Sir Sadiq Khan for his stance on the dispute.

Addressing the TUC Congress in Brighton on Wednesday, Dempsey condemned the Mayor of London for negotiating via social media and spouting “old, tired clichés”.

His message to Sir Keir Starmer was even more stark. Dempsey warned that the RMT has no plans to affiliate with Labour after it split from the party in 2006 and could even throw its weight behind the party being formed by Jeremy Corbyn.

“We supported him when he was in Labour and we supported him when he was an independent,” he told the PoliticsJoe podcast.

Dempsey said he had no idea and did not care who was running for the Labour deputy leadership and that RMT members “are not that interested”.

He said: “It’s no wonder people are drifting away, if you want people to support you, you’ve got to deal with working class issues. We’re interested in results. It’s transactional. If you are doing a good job for our members we’ll back you, if not we’ll have a row with you.”

One union insider said Dempsey had been “the big hit at the TUC”.

The official said: “Eddie has got 10,000 people out on strike, which helps, but people know him from his double act with Mick Lynch during the last strikes a few years ago. He’s not doing this to be a celebrity, but he has a big profile and it’s going to get bigger.”

Dempsey grew up in the tough environment of the Woodpecker Estate in New Cross and is a supporter of Millwall Football Club – whose fans revel in the chant “no one likes us, we don’t care” – as was Crow, whom Dempsey has cited as a major influence.

Like Lynch and Mick Cash, his two immediate predecessors, he also comes from an Irish Catholic background, which played a formative role in his politics as he was influenced by socialist figures like James Connolly.

Prior to his high-profile role in the RMT, Dempsey was London secretary of the Connolly Association, the oldest migrant workers’ organisation in Britain and named after James Connolly, an Irish republican who was executed during the 1916 Easter Rising.

He said in a 2019 blog post that he had been introduced to Marxism by “veteran comrades” such as Communist Party activist Monty Goldman and Max Levitas, an Irish-born communist campaigner in London’s East End.

Dempsey’s first job was as a trader at Deptford market, where he honed some of the negotiating skills he later brought to bear at the RMT.

He later moved on to the railway, first as a platform worker and then as a train driver, before rising through the ranks of the RMT as it engaged in numerous disputes over pay, perks and attempts to cut jobs.

He also played a major part in the RMT’s campaign to leave the European Union, which the union distrusted over its stance on organised labour and moves to encourage privatisation.

Advancing to assistant general secretary, he accompanied Lynch on media rounds during 2022 and 2023 strikes in which the RMT was perceived to have got the better of Tory ministers and ultimately won a new pay deal.

Dempsey made headlines in March when he said he would celebrate becoming leader of the RMT by playing bingo at his local social club with his wife, and was reported to be living in a council house in Islington, north London, despite a £100,000 pay package.

Dempsey also hit out at Labour MP Clive Lewis, who he claimed had compared him to Sir Oswald Mosley. He said he had grown up in one of the most diverse estates in Britain and that his political heroes had fought fascism and later opposed Enoch Powell.

With Londoners’ goodwill fast evaporating, Dempsey’s Tube strike is a major gamble. He may have left Deptford, but he’s still a trader at heart.

data.title: ‘Save British double-deckers from cheap Chinese imports’
data.description:
China is threatening to steal Britain’s crown as a leader in electric bus manufacturing with an influx of cheap imports, the head of the country’s biggest bus-maker has warned.

Jean-Marc Gales, chief executive of Wrightbus, said Chinese rivals such as BYD and Yutong were receiving huge state subsidies from Beijing, making them a threat to home-grown UK manufacturers unless urgent action was taken by the Government.

Gales said a lower level of subsidy funding from the Government, one that had served to encourage British bus operators and councils to buy from Wrightbus, had all but dried up – leaving the company vulnerable to Chinese state-subsidised competition.

“What we want is a level playing field, not one where competitors from a particular country get better conditions than all of the others,” he said.

“That does not mean protectionism, but it does mean support. We’re one of the only countries that doesn’t support its own industry in terms of buses.”

He pointed to the example of France, which he said had “an inferior” bus industry that continued to thrive thanks to a requirement for 45pc of all components to be European.

“How about a 40pc UK content to protect this industry? But we never do,” he said.

Wrightbus, based in Northern Ireland, is owned by Jo Bamford, son of JCB chairman Lord Bamford.

It was saved from bankruptcy by Bamford in 2019 and has expanded to 2,300 employees from just 49.

Since then, Wrightbus has done what most other automotive firms have found impossible in overtaking Chinese firm BYD to become the world leader in electric double-deckers after slashing costs to within 5pc of its Asian rival.

But UK financial support is fast evaporating. Remaining funding schemes exclude the retrofitting of diesel bus fleets to run on batteries, depriving Wrightbus of sales of its newest technology while ruling out one of the most affordable routes to cutting carbon emissions for local authorities.

Gales, 62, said the imbalance threatened to limit growth at Wrightbus and opened the way for China to dominate in zero-emission buses just as it does in electric cars.

He said continued success cannot be guaranteed without some measure of government support and incentives.

Wrightbus has repeatedly pressed the case in meetings with Simon Lightwood, the buses minister, he said.

“We talk with them all the time. They listen and they acknowledge, but so far nothing more. We hope that very soon there is another programme because we are ready,” he added.

‘How long can we win?’

Gales, an automotive veteran who led the turnaround of Lotus and was previously second-in-command at Peugeot-Citroen, said the success of Wrightbus since its rescue from insolvency meant it had the potential to become a rare UK export success story.

Annual revenues are expected to reach £600m this year from deliveries of 1,180 buses, 90pc of them electric. This is more than three times the level in 2022, the year before Gales became boss, making it the fastest-growing bus manufacturer in Europe.

Bamford has set a revenue target of £1bn, something the chief executive said could be achieved by reducing the time it took to build a bus by a third while stepping up overseas sales.

However, Gales said that Wrightbus, which is based in Ballymena, County Antrim, needed a strong home market to back up export drives in countries including Singapore, Hong Kong and Australia.

He said: “We can become a world champion for Britain in electric buses. We’ve shown with double-deckers that we can do it. BYD are a giant and we have taken that crown.

“We have the same inventory levels that we had two years ago, but we are building three times as many buses. We use aluminium when the Chinese use steel, our reliability is higher, we have far better support networks and Northern Ireland is competitive on pay.

“But if people are going to take you seriously as an exporter you have to be number one at home. And if there is unfair competition, how long can we win?”

Chinese firms already benefit from cheaper labour and huge economies of scale – the market requires 100,000 new buses a year compared with just 2,000 in the UK – even before the impact of state subsidies is taken into account.

Moreover, China still builds many diesel buses and coaches for domestic use, doing nothing to reduce carbon emissions, while electric models are directed at markets such as the UK.

The British city-bus sector is already dominated by electric vehicles (EVs), which account for around 70pc of annual sales, a far greater proportion than for cars, where penetration stands at just 20pc.

Wrightbus controls slightly more than one third of the overall UK new bus market, with seven or eight other manufacturers active, including Chinese firms with about 20pc. Yet increasing that share significantly might not be possible, said Gales.

In response to that reality Wrightbus has developed its conversion division, which allows a diesel bus halfway through its working life of some 18 years to become zero-emission for around £200,000 – or half the price of a new EV bus.

Lorries of the future

In a further development of the repowering model, Wrightbus last week unveiled its first electric truck conversion, costing around £115,000.

The brainchild of what Gales called “some of our Freds in the shed”, the plan involves the conversion of 18-tonne lorries weighing the same as double-decker buses and therefore able to use the exact same drivetrain.

Wrightbus launched the business using a DAF model of which there are 30,000 in operation in the UK, representing a potential conversion market almost as big as the country’s 40,000-strong bus fleet. Just 1pc of trucks in Britain are currently battery-powered.

Gales, who also worked for Fiat and ran the Williams Advanced Engineering, an offshoot from the Formula 1 team, said that in the longer term many buses and trucks might move to hydrogen propulsion.

That will be music to the ears of Bamford, one of the world’s leading advocates for hydrogen and founder of Ryze Power and the Hycap hydrogen investment fund.

Fuel-cell technology remains expensive, Gales said, and is better suited to routes that are longer and can potentially command premium fares, such as a Brighton to Gatwick service for which Wrightbus recently won orders.

However, he added that hydrogen combustion could have far greater potential, offering a straightforward alternative to diesel with a range significantly greater than can be achieved with batteries.

That would allow it to power long-distance coach services and HGVs operating the length of Britain and beyond without the inconvenience and financial and time penalties of having to stop to charge up.

He said: “You need modifications like a new cylinder head because the hydrogen is volatile and burns faster. And you need more sensors and electrics to control the burn, and space in the bus for the tanks.

“But that is something where the UK can also become a world leader – with the right support.”

data.title: AstraZeneca stokes fears of London exit with New York listing
data.description:
AstraZeneca has announced plans to list shares on the New York Stock Exchange, stoking fears that Britain’s biggest drugmaker could quit London.

In a shake-up announced on Monday, the FTSE pharmaceuticals giant said it would upgrade its US listing to ensure it is equally as important in New York as it is on the London Stock Exchange.

Bosses said the move would make it easier for the company to raise money from US investors, describing it as “a global listing for global investors in a global company”.

As part of the announcement, the company said it would maintain its London listing and remain headquartered in the UK.

However, this move will stoke fears over AstraZeneca’s future in London. Analysts at AJ Bell said it hinted “at the possibility of a more dramatic shift at some point in the future”.

Mark Kelly, the chief executive of corporate advisory firm MKP Advisors, said he expected AstraZeneca would leave the London market within the next two or three years, adding: “This is a halfway house, it’s potentially a backdoor route to an eventual full US listing.

“I don’t think anyone is necessarily surprised to see AstraZeneca going this way.”

Ketan Patel, a fund manager at AstraZeneca shareholder Whitefriars, said the risk of the company quitting London was a “concern for the UK market”. He said its exit would come as a “huge blow” to UK investors.

Such concerns will no doubt sound alarm bells for the Labour Government, which is currently trying to rally support among businesses at its party conference in Liverpool.

AstraZeneca, which was the UK’s most valuable listed company up until last week, has long been viewed as one of the businesses most likely to ditch its London listing.

Earlier this year, reports suggested that Sir Pascal Soriot, AstraZeneca’s chief executive, was keen to move the company’s main listing to New York – owing to growing frustration with the UK.

Sir Pascal described the drugmaker as a “very American company”.

The business was formed in 1999 through the merger of Sweden’s Astra AB and Britain’s Zeneca PLC. It remains headquartered in Cambridge.

Mr Kelly said: “AstraZeneca is only really here because when they did the original AstraZeneca merger, it made most sense at the time – London was a bastion of financial equity markets, and it was a wonderful place for a massive company to be listed. It’s difficult to make that argument in 2025.”

Mr Patel said: “There is a clear strategy towards a business that is US-focused, and I would not be surprised if a full exit from London is on the cards. The company already reports and pays dividends in US dollars.”

Currently, more than 40pc of AstraZeneca’s revenue comes from the US, making it the company’s largest market by a significant margin. China accounts for 13pc of revenues, while Europe stands at 21pc.

In recent months, AstraZeneca has been stepping up investment in the US – including a $50bn (£37bn) announcement – following demands from Donald Trump, the US president, for companies to invest more there.

At the same time, the pharmaceutical company has pulled a series of investments from Britain amid an ongoing row with ministers over high medicine charges.

Earlier this month, AstraZeneca halted a £200m investment in its Cambridge research facility after the Government walked away from talks to lower drug charges for medicines in the NHS.

This has fuelled claims that Britain is becoming “uninvestable” for drugmakers.

The Cambridge move followed an earlier decision by AstraZeneca to walk away from a £450m investment in a Liverpool vaccine factory in protest over the Government reducing funding for the site.

Sean Conroy, an analyst at Shore Capital, said the latest move from AstraZeneca could arguably be “a lever and a negotiation tactic to be able to demonstrate that it is cosying up to the US a bit more” – putting Britain under pressure at the same time.

data.title: Passengers face a decade of delays on London-Scotland rail route
data.description:
Millions of passengers face diversions of two hours or more during a decade of engineering work on Britain’s busiest intercity railway line.

A project to renew 1970s-era overhead wires and signalling equipment on the London to Glasgow route will commence in August next year, Network Rail said.

The work will bring years of disruption to 200 miles of the West Coast Main Line (WCML) north of Crewe as sections of track are closed for weeks at a time.

An indication of the likely impact was provided on Monday when train operator Avanti West Coast said the replacement of a bridge on the line will lead to two and a half-hour detours between Preston to Carlisle for two weeks in January.

The work on the Clifton Bridge, which spans the M6 in Cumbria, will see trains diverted off the 125mph main line onto the remote and non-electrified Settle and Carlisle route, which normally has just eight local passenger trains a day in each direction.

It will stretch the 80-mile journey from Preston to Carlisle to one of almost 120 miles, adding 80 minutes to the usual travel time, excluding a longer wait at either end as the service is expected to run only every two hours.

Avanti said it will use the detour to prepare for the years of regular closures beginning later in 2026.

Avanti, which is due to be nationalised in October 2026, said the re-routing will depend on the areas of the WCML that are closed, as well as factors such as the time of year and anticipated passenger loads.

It said the work will deliver a “once-in-a-generation” modernisation that will drive up train performance and boost economic growth.

Phil James, Network Rail’s route director for the North West, said the diversions will allow more people to complete their journeys without transferring to cars and buses, though rail replacement buses will still be required to reach Lancaster and two stations in Cumbria.

“We work together as a rail industry to keep passengers on trains and limit disruption as much as possible during our planned engineering upgrades,” he said.

January’s work will see a 60-year-old bridge over which only one train can pass at a time replaced with a 500-foot, 3,000-tonne structure costing £60m.

In a taste of things to come, the company said people travelling from north-west England will have to choose between making the detour or taking a slow train across the Pennines to reach Scotland by way of the East Coast Mine Line.

Passengers wanting to travel to Glasgow from London will be advised to abandon the WCML completely and use the East Coast line throughout, extending the trip to almost five hours, as will those from Birmingham, via a lengthy journey across the Midlands.

While Avanti runs the most trains on the WCML, carrying 35 million passengers in the year through March, the northern reaches of the route are also used by operators including Northern, TransPennine, ScotRail, Transport for Wales and the Caledonian Sleeper.

data.title: Hit drivers with pay-per-mile tax to fix Budget, Reeves urged
data.description:
Drivers should be taxed for every mile they travel to help Rachel Reeves fix a hole in Britain’s public finances, a leading think tank has urged.

The Resolution Foundation, which has considerable influence in Labour circles, said vehicle taxes should be overhauled to replace lost fuel duty as more drivers switch from petrol and diesel cars to electric vehicles (EVs).

The think tank has proposed a system to hit drivers with an annual tax plus another fee of between 3p and 9p per mile driven. Drivers of heavier vehicles could be charged the higher price, reflecting wear and tear on Britain’s roads.

“Motoring taxes are an important part of the tax system but they are also an obvious and significant fiscal risk,” said Adam Corlett, who wrote the report.

It’s feared the Chancellor will have to raise as much as £30bn in taxes in her November Budget.

Taxing drivers per mile could raise up to £20bn per year for the Government, the Resolution Foundation suggested.

Fuel duty revenues are set to fall by one fifth by the end of this decade compared to five years ago, from £28bn to £22.6bn, according to the Office for Budget Responsibility.

That drop comes as drivers switch to EVs as well as to hybrid cars and more efficient traditional petrol and diesel models.

Mr Corlett suggested the lightest cars be charged 3p, rising to 9p per mile for heavier models.

“Distance could be logged via a mixture of MOTs, self-reporting and ideally telematics (with the latter helping to exclude miles driven outside the UK and perhaps facilitating local per-mile additions in future to tackle the worst congestion),” his report said.

He also proposes cutting VAT on public chargers from 20pc to 5pc to match the tax on home electricity bills as well as increasing taxes on non-electric cars.

The Resolution Foundation estimates the changes would raise £2bn per year by the end of the decade, £10bn in 2035 and £20bn per year by 2040.

Motoring groups oppose road pricing. Ian Taylor, from the Alliance of British Drivers, said pay per mile schemes are not “particularly fair”.

“It would almost certainly put prices up and probably the only way to administer it is to track everyone, which has freedom and privacy implications,” he said, noting options include roadside cameras or satellites.

“It costs a lot of money to administer, regardless of how you do it.”

Mr Corlett also proposed ending the freeze on fuel duty, which has not increased since 2011. He said the tax should rise by 3pc every year.

Similarly the temporary 5p cut, which is due to expire in March, could be reversed gradually over a number of years to ease the impact “when concern about inflation is likely to still be high”.

This change would see the tax rise from 52.95p per litre currently to almost 70p by the end of the decade.

Petrol cost an average of 134p per litre last week, according to the Department for Transport. That is up by just over 6pc from around 126p before the pandemic – a noticeable increase for drivers.

A Treasury spokesman said: “The Chancellor makes tax policy decisions at fiscal events. We do not comment on speculation around future changes to tax policy.”

data.title: Ryanair accused of ‘disgraceful’ attitude to older passengers
data.description:
Ryanair has been accused of ignoring elderly passengers after confirming plans to scrap paper boarding passes.

A Ryanair shake-up, due to take effect in November, will result in 40 million Ryanair customers who use paper tickets being forced to carry passes on a smartphone, which campaigners said will make travelling harder for older passengers.

Silver Voices, an over-60s lobby group, branded the decision a “disgraceful move” and said the low-cost airline was putting profit ahead of the well-being of customers.

Age UK said it will disadvantage the four million Britons who have never used the internet and the millions more who lack an Android or Apple mobile phone.

On Wednesday, Ryanair said it will adopt digital-only ticketing from Nov 12, meaning “passengers will no longer be able to download and print a physical paper boarding pass”.

It said that travellers “will instead need to use the digital boarding pass generated in their ‘myRyanair’ app during check-in to board their Ryanair flight”.

Ryanair, led by Michael O’Leary, will become the first major European airline to ban paper passes, which are still accepted at British Airways, easyJet, Jet2, Wizz Air and TUI.

Impact on customers

Dennis Reed, director of Silver Voices, accused Ryanair of seeking to “cut costs and cut corners without caring about the impact on their customers”.

He said: “It’s a disgraceful move. They are effectively saying they don’t want older people as passengers. There’s a strong argument to say that it’s discriminatory.”

He argued that many older people do not have smartphones, while those who do may not know how to download an app or have the physical capacity to manipulate a small device, whether due to eyesight issues, arthritis or some form of disability.

He added: “This is going to cause chaos. If somebody goes to the airport to catch a Ryanair flight without the app, what are they going to do? Turn them away?

“It’s also going to isolate older people from society, which is what businesses that go completely digital do.

“I really would urge them to reconsider. I, for one, will not be flying with Ryanair, and I would urge other people to take the same action.”

Caroline Abrahams, charity director at Age UK, said companies should not ignore those with limited digital skills when updating their ticketing processes.

She said: “There should always be an alternative way of booking and showing tickets that does not disadvantage those who aren’t online.”

Ryanair originally planned to scrap paper boarding passes over the summer but shelved the idea amid concerns that it would create confusion and delays at the busiest time for travel.

The airline later said the change would take effect on Nov 3, but has pushed it back by a further nine days to avoid introducing it during the busy half-term school break.

Dara Brady, Ryanair’s chief marketing officer, said the new timing should “ensure a seamless transition” to 100pc digital boarding passes, which he said would mean “a faster, smarter and greener travel experience for our customers”.

Ryanair said the switch should also eliminate almost all airport check-in fees, as passengers will need to check in online or via the app to generate their digital pass.

It said that almost 80pc of its 200 million passengers already use digital boarding passes, and that going paper-free will save over 300 tonnes in waste each year.

The airline claimed that for passengers with impaired vision, the app has the advantage of gathering information in one place, with screen readers able to read the details aloud – something it said “cannot be completed with outdated paper boarding passes”.

It said passengers who have checked in and downloaded a boarding pass will be assisted free of charge at the airport if their phone subsequently dies or is lost.

data.title: Northern Powerhouse Rail to be delayed again in blow to Starmer
data.description:
Northern Powerhouse Rail is to be delayed again, in a blow to Sir Keir Starmer.

Concerns over the cost of the plan to build a rail line between Liverpool and Manchester have led it to be dropped from the Prime Minister’s conference speech.

It had been set to be the flagship announcement at Labour’s annual gathering in Liverpool next week.

Sources told the BBC, which first reported the delay, that it was an attempt to avoid similar mistakes to those made by HS2, which has repeatedly gone over budget.

Officials were said to be particularly concerned about whether connecting Manchester airport to the city centre, a part of Northern Powerhouse Rail that overlapped with part of the cancelled HS2 phase 2, still represented value for money.

Sir Keir had been hoping to use his conference speech to relaunch his premiership after a torrid summer.

He has lost Angela Rayner, former deputy prime minister, after a Telegraph investigation into her tax affairs, while Lord Mandelson, his ambassador to the US, was forced to resign over his links to Jeffrey Epstein.

Morgan McSweeney, his most trusted adviser, is also facing questions over late payments from a think tank he helped set up to the Electoral Commission.

Andy Burnham, whom some Labour MPs are urging to challenge Sir Keir for the Labour leadership, has long been a champion of the project in his job as Manchester mayor.

In response to the plans being delayed again, a source close to Burnham told the BBC: “Any more tyre kicking and there will be no tyre left.”

Northern Powerhouse Rail was initially floated by George Osborne in 2014 as a means of rebalancing the economy, and was projected to cost around £43bn.

Initial plans would have seen the line stretching between Newcastle, Hull, Sheffield and Liverpool, linking to the since-cancelled eastern spur of HS2.

Revisions by Jeremy Hunt in 2022 saw the plans cut back to a new, £17bn line between Liverpool and Manchester.

In June, Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, said she would “set out” plans to “take forward our ambitions for Northern Powerhouse Rail… in the coming weeks”.

Since the spending review, a black hole of up to £50bn has emerged in the public finances, which she is expected to fill with a swathe of tax rises.

The Labour-linked Resolution Foundation think tank urged the Chancellor earlier this week to cut National Insurance by 2p and add it to income tax.

Another suggestion from the Resolution Foundation, whose former chief executive Torsten Bell is now a Treasury minister after being selected for a safe Labour seat in 2024, is a pay-per-mile tax on motorists.

A Conservative spokesman said: “Yet again it’s more big talk, and no real plans from this Labour Government.

“By bowing down to their union paymasters with inflation-busting pay rises Labour sent the signal that their donors come first, and passengers second – and the fiasco of nationalisation has been proof of that.”

Paul Kohler, the Liberal Democrats’ transport spokesman, said: “This setback delays the vital boost to northern communities that they have been promised.

“The Government must move quickly to resolve these issues, stop the delays and get on with the job. At the moment, this project is just as plagued by delays as actual trains.”

A government source said ministers remain “fully committed to Northern Powerhouse Rail”.

“We are determined to learn from the mistakes of the past and not repeat the previous government’s failures over HS2 and the botched Network North plan.

“That’s why we’re taking the time to get this right. We will set out our plans in the coming weeks”, they said.

data.title: David Beckham lands £25m payday
data.description:
Sir David Beckham is in line for a $34m (£25m) payout after advertising tie-ups with the likes of Stella Artois and Hugo Boss delivered a record year for his brand.

DRJB Holdings, the holding company that oversees the former footballer’s separate brand and TV production business, paid dividends of $75.7m to shareholders including Sir David, according to newly-filed accounts.

The former Manchester United star, who was knighted this year for services to sport and charity, is entitled to 45pc of the payout through his company Footwork Productions, which owns a minority stake in DRJB Holdings.

The remaining 55pc goes to US sports branding giant Authentic Brands, which bought a portion of DRJB Holdings for $269m three years ago. It will also receive a separate $5.6m dividend because it owns preference shares.

The dividend is an increase from the total $67.4m paid out last year.

The latest figures show the former England captain is continuing to cash in from his personal brand even after ceding control of the business.

Through brand management arm DB Ventures, which is owned by DRJB Holdings, Sir David has struck lucrative marketing deals with brands including SharkNinja and Stella.

He is expected to sign further sponsorship deals in the run-up to the 2026 Fifa World Cup – following a partnership with US telecoms group Verizon.

The 50-year-old also founded Inter Miami FC and has more than 160 million followers on social media.

DRJB Holdings controls Studio 99, the production business behind the 2023 Beckham documentary series for Netflix. Another documentary about his wife Victoria, the fashion designer and former Spice Girl, is due to be released on Netflix next week.

DRJB Holdings reported a pre-tax profit of $45m last year – an increase of almost a quarter. Revenues rose 1pc to just over $92m.

The company said the David Beckham brand had grown “consistently” since the investment from Authentic Brands.

It added: “2024 saw double-digit profit growth as the brand continued to thrive, securing partnerships with world-leading brands whilst entering into innovative new business ventures. The group’s growth momentum has continued into 2025.”

The figures suggest Sir David’s business interests have been unaffected by a high-profile feud within his family.

Eldest son Brooklyn, 26, and his wife Nicola Peltz, an actress and daughter of billionaire activist investor Nelson Peltz, have failed to attend a number of family events this year, including Sir David’s 50th birthday celebrations.

Authentic Brands, which is led by Canadian billionaire Jamie Salter, is home to retail brands including Reebok, Ted Baker and Forever 21.

It also owns the rights to the brand and likeness of individuals including Muhammed Ali and Marilyn Monroe.

The company has embarked on an aggressive acquisition spree in recent years and has been exploring a potential stock market float.

data.title: A ‘taxi tax’ will only hurt the vulnerable. Labour has truly lost the plot
data.description:
“Policies like the taxi tax,” Charlie Gilkes tells me, “destroy the ecosystem in which the hospitality sector functions”.

Mr Gilkes, the co-founder of the Inception Group which owns bars and restaurants across London, explains why it isn’t just the taxi industry which is on high alert following rumours that VAT will be added to all cab journeys.

Hospitality was dealt a devastating blow by the Chancellor’s Budget last year, particularly with the change in employers’ National Insurance contributions. In the nine months that followed the industry lost 89,000 jobs, according to the trade body UKHospitality, which is nearly half of all job losses seen in the wider economy.

A 1.2pc rise in NICs means that the earnings threshold at which employers start to be charged dropped from £9,100 to £5,000 a year, bringing even very low-paid part-time employees into the remit of this tax for the first time.

It is not difficult to see why this policy has hit the industry, which is “a leading creator of part-time jobs, first jobs for young people, older people and people returning to the jobs market”, especially hard.

Against what is already a very challenging backdrop, a taxi tax would hit hospitality businesses even harder, right across the nation. Country publicans are concerned about higher taxi fares putting off patrons. Staff members, particularly women, often rely on taxi shares in the absence of night buses. These workers will face a rise in the cost of their journey home after a late shift.

A large proportion of those who use taxis are vulnerable people, Layla Barke-Jones, a partner at Aaron and Partners Solicitors tells me. They could be the elderly who are unable to drive themselves or those with a disability who find it difficult to use public transport. “These are the people who would be disproportionately hit,” Ms Barke-Jones warns.

Does this mean local authorities – who are increasingly facing scrutiny for their use of private taxis for residents or even asylum seekers – would have to pay the taxi tax too, passing on an additional burden on to taxpayers?

According to Jonathan Main, a VAT expert and partner at professional services firm MHA, the answer to that question, thankfully, is no. Local authorities belong to a separate VAT regime, which allows them to recover any VAT costs incurred in the process of carrying out their statutory duties.

However, if the taxi tax pushes up the cost of cab journeys, not just by the 20pc levied in VAT, but considerably more than that as operators and taxi drivers grapple with the resulting administrative costs, then it’s difficult to see how that extra cost would not be passed on to all consumers.

And that brings us to the group whose livelihoods would be the most directly threatened by this rumoured tax: taxi drivers themselves.

A spokesman for Bolt, the taxi operating service, says: “They are self-employed entrepreneurs, and around 70pc work outside London. A flat 20pc VAT would inevitably raise costs for consumers and reduce demand, leading to a drop in driver earnings.

“We believe VAT should be applied fairly on operators’ margins [as is already the case], not on the full fare, to protect drivers’ livelihoods and keep rides affordable for passengers.”

It is clear that the Budget in late November will be the frostiest yet. A ruinous taxi tax will only prolong the winter.

data.title: Tony Blair’s billionaire ally shifting the world’s media to the Right
data.description:
Billionaire Larry Ellison may be one of the world’s richest men, but for decades he was overshadowed by tech luminaries like Bill Gates, Steve Jobs, Mark Zuckerberg and Elon Musk.

Not any more.

The 81-year-old tycoon, whose net worth briefly overtook Musk’s earlier this month, has become a pivotal force in the global media and tech landscape, capturing the zeitgeist of Donald Trump’s tech-dominated administration.

While Oracle, his software giant, may not be a household name, hopes of an AI boom have propelled the company into territory reserved for the likes of the “magnificent seven” – Apple, Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, Meta, Tesla and Nvidia. Shares rose an astonishing 43pc last week after a positive trading update.

Now Ellison – a long-time ally of former prime minister Sir Tony Blair – is using his billions to reshape the media landscape to his own whims, teeing up a shift to the Right by America’s TV, film and tech industries.

Most recently, the tycoon has also been bankrolling his son David’s media venture Skydance. It recently clinched an $8bn (£6bn) takeover of Channel 5 owner Paramount and has already set its sights on Hollywood behemoth Warner Bros Discovery.

Meanwhile, Ellison is a linchpin in the conglomerate which could buy up TikTok’s US assets after Trump ordered a sale of the Chinese social media giant.

Recently asked who should buy TikTok, Trump responded: “I’d like Larry to buy it.”

This flurry of dealmaking means Ellison is poised to become one of the most powerful media moguls in the world. And as he cosies up to Trump, he looks poised to play a key role in a fundamental shake-up of US media.

Ellison, who founded Oracle in California in 1977, belongs to a different breed of tech entrepreneurs from the outspoken Silicon Valley tycoons of today.

Despite making billions from his software company, in which he holds a 41pc stake, the businessman has largely kept himself out of the public eye, rarely giving interviews and keeping his social media presence to a minimum.

Instead, Ellison has had fun. From his successful yachting escapades in the Americas Cup to buying up Hawaii’s sixth-largest island, he has demonstrated extravagant tastes.

He has spent an estimated $875m on the Oracle racing team, which has won the Americas Cup twice, and around $200m on a California estate modelled on a 16th-century imperial Japanese palace.

Ellison invested $1bn in Musk’s 2022 takeover of Twitter because, he said, it “would be lots of fun”. He has been married six times, with his latest wife Jolin Zhu almost 50 years his junior.

He also has a footprint in the UK, establishing a major new research centre in Oxford named the Ellison Institute of Technology and purchasing The Eagle and Child pub, a historic drinking hole favoured by writers including CS Lewis and JRR Tolkien.

This empire-building in Oxford is separate to Oracle’s pledge to invest $5bn in data centres across the country. These ambitious plans come as the rapid growth of AI has sparked renewed interest in Oracle and sent Ellison’s personal wealth soaring.

But it is his moves in the media sector that have put the tycoon firmly in the spotlight, raising questions over how Ellison’s politics will influence the sector.

Media play

Through Skydance’s takeover of Paramount, which was also funded in part by private equity firm RedBird Capital, he has gained a foothold in a media empire that owns Channel 5 and CBS.

Should the proposed swoop on Warner Bros Discovery be successful, this influence would expand to one of the world’s most prestigious film studios, as well as HBO and CNN.

Meanwhile, the possible deal for TikTok, which is also backed by Marc Andreessen’s venture capital firm a16z, means Ellison could be a key player in a trend-setting social media platform that has an audience of almost 150 million in the US. The deal is yet to be finalised, with the US and China clashing over the progress of the deal on Friday.

Taken together, these investments put Ellison at the heart of some of the world’s most powerful outlets in both traditional and new media – giving him power over cross-generational platforms that would be the envy of any media mogul.

This newfound power raises questions about Ellison’s political leanings. A former supporter of Bill Clinton, the tycoon has also donated hundreds of millions of dollars to Blair’s non-profit institute.

More recently, though, the pragmatic Ellison has aligned himself with Trump. In 2020, he hosted a fundraiser for the US president at his California estate. It later emerged that he joined a call discussing strategies for contesting Trump’s election defeat.

There are already signs that Ellison’s newly-acquired media outlets are shifting to the Right.

Paramount-owned CBS sacked late-night host Stephen Colbert days after he described the parent company’s decision to settle a lawsuit with Trump as a “big fat bribe”.

Skydance also vowed to shun diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI) policies to help secure approval for the merger from the Trump administration, while it has now installed an ally of the president in the newly created role of “ombudsman” at CBS News.

Skydance is now said to be in discussions about buying The Free Press, an online media outlet founded by the high-profile “anti-woke” journalist Bari Weiss. It is thought that Weiss would be handed a senior role at CBS as part of a deal worth at least $100m.

Trump tightens grip

Whether Ellison’s takeover of the US media – and the ensuing shift to the Right – is born of ideology or merely a pragmatic approach to protecting his business interests remains unclear.

But it comes as Trump tightens his grip over his liberal media foes.

Disney last week took Jimmy Kimmel off air following his comments about the assassination of Charlie Kirk – a move cheered by the US president.

Meanwhile, the Right-leaning stance of Murdoch’s outlets has been ensured following the ascension of eldest son Lachlan to the helm of the empire.

“You’ve got this very intriguing parallel movement taking place,” says Alex DeGroote, a media analyst.

“Trump is knocking off one by one all the late-night TV show hosts, but at the same time the actual ownership of the networks is changing as well ... There’s a sort of conservativisation taking place of big US media.”

The redrawing of the US media landscape goes beyond Ellison as Trump opportunistically seeks to consolidate his power.

The octogenarian has waited decades to come out of the shadows of the Silicon Valley messiahs. Now it’s his turn to call the shots.

data.title: GSK boss says US is ‘biggest priority’ as Trump pressures drugmakers
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drugmaker has said the US is its “biggest priority” amid pressure from Donald Trump to slash prices for US citizens.

Dame Emma Walmsley, who will step down from GSK at the end of the year, praised the US and said the group is working with the Trump administration after it launched a campaign to address high drug prices.

Shares in pharmaceutical companies surged on Tuesday after rival pharma group Pfizer struck a deal with the White House to lower prices for some of its medicines. Mr Trump has also demanded greater investment in the US from drug companies after introducing a 100pc tariff on branded drugs made abroad.

The Pfizer agreement, which averts tariffs for three years, triggered a relief rally that sent shares in AstraZeneca up 11.2pc, as it reclaimed its place as Britain’s most valuable company. GSK shares jumped by 6.2pc.

Dame Emma admitted GSK was “betting big on the US” after she pledged during Mr Trump’s UK state visit last month to invest $30bn (£22bn) in America over the next five years.

She said: “Absolutely GSK is engaging constructively. The markets also view it this way, as it’s about constructive dialogue between the industry and the Government.

“The US is by far the biggest priority market for our industry because the US is the best country in the world to discover and develop innovation.”

As the country’s largest drugmaker, GSK has become a national champion for the UK pharmaceutical sector, despite generating only a small proportion of its revenues in the UK.

Britain represents just 2pc of the £65bn drugmaker sales, compared to 52pc in the US.

Despite this, Dame Emma said the group would not ditch its listing on the London stock market for New York. GSK’s shares have fallen overall in the eight years since she took over in 2017.

“I’m not getting distracted by the listing questions that everyone keeps asking about,” Dame Emma told Bloomberg’s Women, Money & Power conference.

“Our listing location doesn’t distract from where we allocate our capital in terms of investments. There are no plans for that.”

She also said there had been “zero engagement” on a potential merger with any other UK pharmaceutical company.

AstraZeneca fears

Her comments come days after AstraZeneca stoked fears that Britain’s biggest drugmaker could quit London after it announced plans to list shares on the New York Stock Exchange.

In a shake-up announced on Monday, the FTSE pharmaceuticals giant said it would upgrade its US listing to ensure it is equally as important in New York as it is on the London Stock Exchange.

Bosses said the move would make it easier for the company to raise money from US investors, describing it as “a global listing for global investors in a global company”.

Analysts at AJ Bell said the decision hinted “at the possibility of a more dramatic shift at some point in the future”.

data.title: Shoppers warned of £1.6bn in price rises
data.description:
Shoppers have been told to brace for £1.6bn of price rises this year as supermarkets grapple with a new net zero tax.

High street bosses said they would have to pass on to consumers more than 80pc of the cost from a recycling tax, which is coming into force this week.

It means shoppers will see prices rising by as much as £1.6bn from the new tax alone, with the scheme expected to cost retailers and manufacturers around £2bn a year.

Andrew Opie, from industry group the British Retail Consortium (BRC), said this would be a “multibillion-pound levy being paid by consumers during a cost of living crisis”, adding: “They will ask: what are we getting for higher prices?”

It comes amid growing concerns over the risk of piling extra costs on retailers, as store prices start to climb.

Figures this week revealed that the pace of shop price rises stepped up in September, increasing 1.4pc year on year versus a 0.9pc annual rise for August.

The Bank of England earlier this month said government policy was partly to blame for higher inflation. Officials have been battling to bring inflation under control after it hit 3.8pc in August, well above the Bank’s 2pc target.

The extended producer responsibility (EPR) scheme, which is kicking in from October, is designed to force producers of food and drink packaging to pay for its disposal. The Government said it hoped to raise £1bn a year from the policy.

However, retailers and food and drink-makers have warned that they cannot swallow the higher costs. The BRC said retailers were already grappling with an extra £5bn in employment costs after the Chancellor raised their National Insurance contributions and National Living Wage in her last Budget.

It added: “This new tax will be yet another inflationary pressure, at a time when food prices are already rising fast.”

The Bank of England estimates that EPR will add to food inflation. Under the policy, the amount of tax will be calculated by weight, as well as the difficulty and expense of collection and disposal.

It means the levy will be higher for heavier items such as glass bottles, than for lighter packaging, such as plastic or aluminium.

Sean Murphy, managing director at glass giant Encirc, said the changes threatened to send the price of products in glass bottles or jars soaring.

Estimates this summer suggested that the policy would mean a standard bottle of wine would cost 9p more and the cost of a 330ml beer bottle will rise by around 4p.

Mr Murphy said: “The new glass bottle tax is an economic and environmental own goal, hitting UK firms producing packaging that is far better for the environment than plastic.”

He added: “This tax will hit everyone – in the supermarkets, pubs and glass factories that create thousands of jobs.”

The Government has said the scheme is intended to encourage manufacturers to use environmentally friendly materials.

The BRC has called for the levy to be ringfenced to improve local recycling.

Mr Opie said: “Unless funds are spent transparently and effectively, EPR threatens to just be another burden on an already overtaxed industry with no tangible benefits for customers or the environment.”

The Government said: “EPR moves the cost of dealing with waste away from taxpayers, generates over a £1bn annually that must be spent by councils to improve recycling collections and thereby benefit every household across England.

“The changes underpin major investment in the UK economy, helping create 25,000 jobs and we will continue to listen and work with trade and industry bodies as they are implemented.”

data.title: Trump’s TikTok deal sets a very dangerous precedent
data.description:
With his glamorous first lady at his side, the president boasted how an “economically free, politically sovereign” nation was emerging from the chaos and corruption of the recent past.

Top of his achievements so far, he boasted, was taming an irresponsible media.

This wasn’t Donald Trump, however, but Juan Perón, the president of Argentina, speaking in 1950.

Nicknamed El Primer Trabajador – the “first worker” – Perón rejected global trade and championed the working-class man, like Trump.

Perón also closed 45 newspapers hostile to his administration, suing two of the largest for libel. Like Peron, Trump has also launched lawsuits against the New York Times and the Wall Street Journal for publishing defamatory articles.

As a result, the administration now looks more Peronist than ever, particularly after deposing another critical TV show host.

After Stephen Colbert’s show was pulled in July, Jimmy Kimmel was suspended by ABC last week after Brendan Carr, the chairman of the Federal Communications Commission (FCC), threatened to revoke the broadcaster’s license in the wake of comments by the late night talk show host about the shooting of Charlie Kirk.

Conservative torch-bearer and US senator Ted Cruz later compared the threat to “mafioso” tactics.

But that’s not even the biggest Trump media story of last week. Particularly as only a handful of 45 to 60-year-olds watch late-night US TV: barely over a million in all, in Kimmel’s case, while Colbert’s show was reportedly losing $40m (£29m) a year.

By contrast, something far more valuable is about to fall into the lap of Team Trump: the biggest and most-watched media platform in the world, TikTok.

The proposed deal to solidify American ownership of the Chinese app confirms the emergence of the Ellisons, the dynasty headed by Oracle founder Larry Ellison, as the most powerful media family in the world and one very close to the president.

The family already owns Paramount and CBS, whilst Ellison’s Oracle is now set to also control TikTok as part of a US consortium after Donald Trump struck a deal with China.

But there are two really remarkable aspects to this story which merit more attention.

One is that this is a straightforward asset seizure, which has not gone unnoticed by American executives with investments in China and the BRICS. The other is Silicon Valley’s role in the story.

The internet companies have a dirty secret: they hate competition and are not very good at it.

If you can’t beat them, buy them, and if you can’t buy them, ban them.

Five years ago, TikTok’s owner ByteDance was trouncing Silicon Valley at its own game, with a wildly addictive new video service.

It had effectively created a new medium. TikTok’s algorithm was so compelling that it was being compared to crack cocaine, and users loved it for another reason too: it didn’t splatter them with ads.

TikTok is more like QVC, a shop window, with an affiliate marketing model. It now has two billion users.

Knowing the Silicon Valley executive cadre well, I find the most compelling theory for TikTok’s story to be this.

Global success and the absence of competition had rendered the social media platforms lazy.

They were enmeshed in “technical debt”, which means layers of ageing computer code that are difficult to maintain but also difficult to replace. And they were welded to a model based on advertising.

Think how many PhDs have walked through the doors of Facebook and Google to devote their planet sized brains to arcane matters such as fine-tuning cost-per-click algorithms, only to discover that the punters would rather not see those ads at all.

Competition means consumers can vote with their feet, and off they marched, en masse, to TikTok.

“We observed that our growth slowed down dramatically,” Zuckerberg said earlier this year regarding the rise of TikTok. So what to do?

The usual answer to an upstart is to make it an offer it can’t refuse. But there was a problem. As ByteDance was a Beijing-based company registered in the Cayman Islands, buying TikTok wasn’t a ready option.

Time to break the glass and pull the emergency cord called national security.

“A decade ago, almost all the major internet platforms were American,” Mark Zuckerberg told an audience in 2019. “Today, six of the top 10 are Chinese.”

The spooks themselves were more ambivalent, however.

They had much bigger fish to fry, such as Huawei, which had embedded itself in much of our critical telecommunications infrastructure.

They let this be known, via the New York Times. If TikTok were a national security threat, why let it continue in any form at all?

Banning TikTok became Plan A. Trump passed an executive order in 2019 to shut it down, and this came into force just as he was being sworn in for the second time.

Shutting down a superior rival is good, but acquiring its secret sauce is even better, and so it has landed, fairly intact, in the hands of new American owners.

This is just the sort of deal Trump likes: his friends get something, the addicts get their digital drug back, and he looks like a hero. Yet it’s all a little too neat. There are grave dangers for US Inc in seizing competitors’ business assets.

Silicon Valley is already playing the national security card on AI, having built the wrong kind. Leaning on the government to crush competition by seizing assets sets a dangerous precedent.

American business has $150bn of assets in BRICS countries and around a trillion dollars worth in China. They may conclude that what’s sauce for the goose is sauce for the gander.

data.title: Financial Times writes off 90pc of investment in European tech coverage
data.description:
The Financial Times has written off the majority of an investment into covering European start-ups amid a downturn in tech funding on the Continent.

The FT said in recently filed accounts that it spent £614,000 on raising its stake in Sifted, a news site aimed at tech entrepreneurs and investors, from 14.4pc to 17.5pc in June 2024.

However, accounts for the same year show it cut the value of its investment by £550,000 just months later. It means almost 90pc of the money pumped into Sifted was effectively wiped out within months.

Touted as a “critical friend of the European start-up ecosystem”, Sifted was launched by John Thornhill, FT’s innovation editor, in 2019 and was backed financially by the newspaper.

Launched as a free news service, Sifted put a paywall in place in 2021. Last year it claimed to be reaching an audience of 120,000 with its daily newsletters and said subscribers to its “pro” offering were up 32pc year on year.

The latest accounts for Sifted show it reduced its headcount from 56 staff members in 2023 to 47 in 2024.

The FT said in its accounts: “Following the completion of the annual impairment review exercise, management concluded that the carrying value of Sifted (EU) Limited could not be supported and the decision was made to record an impairment charge of £555,000.”

Last month, the FT confirmed it would close down The Next Web, an annual tech conference held in Amsterdam, following a strategic review.

The FT had taken a stake in The Next Web, which also operates a news site and was founded in 2006, six years ago as part of a push to “deepen the FT’s reach into the European technology community”.

Digital publishers have been grappling with intense pressure on web traffic driven by changes in search from artificial intelligence. Many are attempting to shift to subscription-based business models.

Other media organisations have also been paring back their coverage of European technology businesses.

TechCrunch, the US news brand, laid off its 10-strong European editorial team in May. Its publisher, private equity firm Regent, insisted it was not “retreating from Europe”.

European technology businesses, meanwhile, have seen a downturn in investment from a high point in 2021 driven by low interest rates. Funding into European start-ups was around $45bn (£33bn) in 2024, according to a report from venture capital firm Atomico, down from more than $100bn during the pandemic.

The FT’s overall revenues rose by 2.5pc in 2024 to £454.6m, while pre-tax profit declined marginally to £3.89m, accounts showed.

Subscribers to its print and digital coverage rose to record levels over the year. Print and digital subscribers increased by 6pc to 1.5 million, with digital-only subscriptions rising 6pc to 1.35 million.

However, it posted a slump in advertising revenues, which fell from £151.6m to £146m.

The FT was approached for comment.

data.title: Yours for just £280: An ‘ethically made’ Guardian jumper
data.description:
The Guardian is selling jumpers for £281 after it reported a loss of almost £30m in the most recent financial year.

Lingua Franca, an American luxury clothing brand, has announced the collaboration with the newspaper to highlight the “importance of fiercely independent journalism”.

The jumpers, listed at $380 (£281), incorporate five Guardian phrases embroidered into the clothing, such as “facts are sacred” and “for facts’ sake”.

Other expressions featured on the sweaters include “fiercely independent”, “the whole picture”, and simply “The Guardian”.

It comes after the Guardian’s holding company reported a £28.8m loss after tax for the year ending in March, according to accounts filed at Companies House.

In a separate annual report, the publication said it generated £275.9m in revenues, an £18.1m increase from £257.8m a year earlier, and that its cash outflow fell from £36.5m to £24.3m. It also reported an increase in “recurring supporters” to 1.3m people.

The newspaper enjoys the backing of the £1.2bn Scott Trust endowment fund, which is intended to support it “in perpetuity”.

In its annual report, Ole Jacob Sunde, the chairman of the Scott Trust, said the Guardian’s losses remained a “substantial number”, but that they were driven by “deliberate investment in editorial capabilities and technology”.

The pressure on the newspaper’s finances comes despite efforts to cut the group’s losses in recent years. Last year, it launched a voluntary redundancy programme for its journalists amid pressure on print advertising and web traffic.

It also sold its loss-making Observer newspaper to Tortoise Media late last year, despite anger from some staff over the deal.

Clothing ‘in alignment with Guardian values’

The jumpers – available in Lingua Franca’s New York City store in October and online – come in up to eight different colours. Its “fiercely independent” children’s crewneck, priced at $125 (£93), has already sold out.

The publication said the Lingua Franca clothing collection is “in alignment with the Guardian’s values”, made with a “focus on natural materials, sustainable production and ethical labour”.

The Lingua Franca website said: “The Guardian is fiercely independent. Nobody influences their journalism.

“They believe that facts are sacred and deliver truth, humanity, and global insight to bring people the whole picture, for free.”

A fifth of the profits from the sales will go to the newspaper to support its journalism, with “the mission to advance and inform public discourse and citizen participation around the most pressing issues of our time”.

It follows the release of the Guardian’s month-long US marketing campaign on Monday, which included tag lines such as, “Uncover what’s truly going on” and “Get wind of climate’s biggest winners and losers”, alongside pictures of Donald Trump, wind turbines and severe weather conditions.

Sara Badler, the chief advertising officer in North America for the Guardian, said: “We are so excited about the Guardian’s collaboration with Lingua Franca, demonstrating how brands can reach audiences in ways that are not only impactful but also authentic and lasting.

“It’s a natural extension of our US brand campaign, ‘The whole picture’, featuring the breadth of the Guardian’s global and independent journalism, which audiences have loyally turned to for more than 200 years.”

data.title: Meghan offered show on Magic Radio
data.description:
Magic Radio has offered to make the Duchess of Sussex a disc jockey after she revealed she missed listening to it.

The Duchess, who moved to California in June 2020, said in her Netflix series, With Love, Meghan, that Magic was “one of the things I miss most about the UK”.

Paul Sylvester, content director of Magic, told a RadioCentre conference on Tuesday: “We had conversations with the Sussex team and if the Duchess would like to add radio presenter to her CV there is an offer on the table.”

Magic said it had “been in with her team and offered her a show, if she ever wanted one”.

It was not revealed how much the Duchess was offered, but UK radio presenter salaries are unlikely to be on the same level as her previous media deals.

The Duke and Duchess were reportedly given a $100m (£74m) five-year deal by Netflix in 2020. They also secured a short-lived deal with Spotify, said to be worth £15.5m.

Owned by Bauer Media, Magic plays music released from the 1980s onwards. It is one of the most popular radio networks in the UK, with 3.6 million weekly listeners, according to Rajar.

The offer from Magic comes despite the Duke and Duchess of Sussex’s struggles to establish themselves in the media world.

Their Spotify-commissioned podcast, Archetypes, was axed after just one series, with critics claiming that that the Duchess’s interview style was “cringe-worthy” and involved talking too much about herself.

Bill Simmons, a Spotify boss, lashed out at the pair as “f---ing grifters” after the end of the podcast deal.

The official line from the streaming giant and the Sussexes’ Archewell charity was that they were “proud of the series we made together”.

In April, the Duchess took another stab at podcasting with an eight-part series, Confessions of a Female Founder. The show was widely panned, with reviewers claiming it contained “surface-level waffle” and was “stomach-turning”.

It quickly dropped out of the Spotify 100 list of top podcasts on both sides of the Atlantic.

The Duke and Duchess have now signed a new multi-year deal with Netflix, which is said to be less extensive and less well-paid than the original agreement.

The Sussexes were approached for comment.

data.title: BT boss attacks ‘government-inflicted costs’
data.description:
The chief executive of BT has attacked “government-inflicted costs” for holding back British businesses.

Allison Kirkby said the telecoms giant was lagging behind its European rivals because of punishing costs heaped on the group by the Labour Government.

She said taxes, levies and red tape meant its spending bill was significantly higher than other companies on the continent.

“We pay in business rates, energy levies and other costs associated with regulation and compliance 10 times the amount our peers pay in countries like Germany and the Netherlands,” she told industry conference Connected Britain on Wednesday.

Ms Kirkby, who was appointed chief executive last year, added that BT was “already at peak government-inflicted costs”.

BT previously admitted the 2024 Budget had cost the company an additional £100m, largely because of an increase in employer National Insurance contributions.

Alongside the rise in labour costs, telecoms companies are also grappling with the UK’s high electricity costs. 

British companies pay the highest electricity prices of anywhere in the developed world, according to government figures.

Electricity in the UK is now about 50pc more expensive than in Germany or France and four times more expensive than in the US.

Ms Kirkby said Rachel Reeves’s Budget on November 26 could be “very difficult” for the Chancellor.

Economists have said Ms Reeves must fill an estimated £20bn shortfall in the public finances to rescue her fiscal plans.

Ms Kirkby, who is a government adviser on trade, called for greater fiscal stability and said the UK is “a sluggish, high inflation market at the moment and we absolutely, as a country, need to get back to growth”.

Business lobby groups and companies have warned the Chancellor to avoid raising taxes on businesses in the upcoming Budget, amid a mounting business backlash against the Government over anti-growth policies.

On Wednesday, oil and gas producer EnQuest accused Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, of creating a “self-inflicted wound” if he allowed the decline of the North Sea to accelerate.

Amjad Bseisu, the company’s chief executive, said there had been a 90pc reduction in investment in the North Sea basin over the last decade to about £2bn last year – forcing Britain to go from being a gas exporter to importing half its stocks of the fossil fuel.

The Energy Secretary is poised to reverse Labour’s ban on new oil and gas drilling amid warnings from union bosses that net zero risks crushing British industry.

Mr Bseisu suggested the Chancellor could also help address the gaping hole in the public finances by cutting windfall taxes on the oil and gas sector.

He urged the Chancellor to make a “dramatic change” to the energy profits levy, which imposes a 78pc tax on North Sea profits.

“Even 10 years ago the revenue base was about £14bn which was significantly higher than it is today. The key is growth. The key is having a fiscal regime that actually fosters growth,” he said.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Boots’ biggest problem might just be its billionaire owner
data.description:
Stefano Pessina loves a good deal. Over the years, the Italian billionaire has struck more than a thousand of them to create global pharmacy juggernaut Walgreens Boots Alliance (WBA), which owns the British chemist Boots. In his lauded career, Pessina has garnered a reputation for ruthlessness.

“A deal must have a strategic logic, and it must create value,” he once said. “I am always very cold when I do a deal.”

Last month, though, there was something markedly off in Pessina’s approach. Following the sale of WBA to US private equity group Sycamore Partners, which saw Pessina retain a significant stake, the 84-year-old and Sycamore’s chief, Stefan Kaluzny, announced they would spin off Boots into a standalone business.

The Italian octogenarian struck an unusually sentimental tone. His family, he said, had “supported the company over many years” and were “committed to investing in The Boots Group in order to accelerate its already very successful record of growth”.

On the face of it, Pessina’s passion for Boots looks like a major tailwind for the chain. The UK retail chain is viewed as a much-loved trophy asset for Pessina and his long-term partner, Ornella Barra, who led international operations at Walgreens and is now head of The Boots Group.

But not everyone is convinced. With the retailer effectively under the Italian family’s control for the foreseeable future, some say it means more of the same for the group: namely, underinvestment and too much focus on its pharmacy business at the expense of other, faster-growing areas.

“Stefano and Ornella operate in a very difficult way for people,” claims one Boots insider. Boots, they say, is highly cash-generative, but executives have choked off investment, with more money needed to help support the American operations.

Though disputed by the group, it’s a claim that raises questions over the future direction of Boots.

Over the past three years, WBA has extracted a substantial £1.1bn worth of dividends from the business but has only reinvested £527m back into the group. Some say these dividends would have been better spent on reviving stores rather than enriching investors.

Critics are now claiming that the biggest problem Boots has could now be its billionaire owner.

No one can doubt that Pessina and his family still have grand ambitions for the chain, which was started by John Boot in Nottingham in 1849.

A second run on the stock market 

Plans to hive off Boots have fired the starting gun on a possible stock market float of one of Britain’s best-loved retailers, almost two decades after it quit the London Stock Exchange.

Friends of Pessina say he would love to see Boots back on the stock market, with plans to list shares in the business when the time is right, which could be as early as the end of 2026.

Although Sycamore – which is understood to hold just over 50pc of the business – would probably exit the business through the float, Pessina would be expected to retain his holding.

“Ultimately, they see it as their thing,” says one Boots source. “Stefano has always wanted to keep Boots and I think, for Ornella, it gives her purpose in life.”

Under Pessina and Barra’s stewardship, Boots is in rude health, racking up 17 consecutive quarters of market share growth. Last year, Boots enjoyed a £40m bounce in profits to £450m, with sales rising across both its beauty and health divisions.

Yet their iron grip could also pose a problem. Within Boots, some argue that the past few years have been a testament to the strength of the business, rather than decisions made in the boardroom.

It is a criticism which has long been levelled at the owners of the British high street chain. While under the WBA umbrella, swathes of Boots stores have been left tired and run-down. Today, some insiders suggest more than two-thirds of stores still need investment.

Sources close to Pessina claim that the separation of Boots from WBA will result in more investment in the chain, arguing that Pessina and Sycamore only took control of the business last month. Neither is expected to take any dividends from the business.

Despite this, some remain unconvinced amid claims that it is challenging to convince Pessina and Barra – both of whom have a pharmacy background – to invest in order to capitalise on the strong demand for the brand.

“The problem is they are not retailers,” says one source. “They’re used to the very low-cost world of pharmacy distribution, and I don’t think they’ve ever shaken that mentality.”

That apparent mindset has become all the more damaging due to the huge potential of the British stalwart, some insiders say. They say that Britons’ love for Boots is unparalleled, having been on the high street for more than 175 years.

For example, Boots is seen as being well ahead of its peers in makeup, hair products and skincare. When the US private equity group Apollo considered a takeover of the business in 2022, the plan was to revamp its beauty halls.

Analysts at Fitch also point to Boots as “leading positions across its key product categories of beauty and health and wellness, benefiting from department store closures”, saying this is one of its core strengths.

Beauty queen

Guy Hands, the buyout tycoon who had been close to a deal for Boots in 2007, says the chain still “has a long way to go in terms of growth with the right owner and the right strategy”, adding that, if he were “in the business of doing big deals still, I’d definitely want to look at it”.

Back when he tabled a bid for the company, he saw the opportunity as twofold.

“We did a lot of customer research, and although there were people going in to buy items like sandwiches, it didn’t justify the footprint that it took up in the store, nor, frankly, did the electronic goods and the other bric-a-brac they were selling. So, our view was to reduce the footprint to what was necessary to do a health and beauty business,” he said.

He says there is still clear room for growth in both these areas. “Its ability to put out beauty products at a competitive price and get very high levels of sales has always been extraordinary,” Hands says.

Meanwhile, in healthcare, he says there is scope to expand its reach, boosting the number of services offered to essentially become a third prong of the health service. “In the current situation with the National Health Service, it’s probably riper for that today than it’s ever been,” he says.

Health concerns

For Boots, the health division appears more challenging than the beauty division. In recent years, numerous local pharmacies have been forced to close due to concerns over low reimbursement rates for medications.

Meanwhile, ministers have claimed they want to use pharmacies to help ease pressure on GPs – offering further income streams to Boots. However, this is part of a longer 10-year NHS plan and could take some time to filter through.

All of this will be in the mix as Pessina and Sycamore mull a public float.

A City source suggested that both Pessina and Sycamore may be better off “holding for a couple of years to maximise value”, adding: “An initial public offering probably needs a better market.”

Whatever the case, Pessina has always been clear that to him, Boots represents more than just another deal to be done. “He has always been a reluctant seller of it,” says one insider.

When the Italian billionaire visited London to celebrate Boots’ 175-year anniversary last November, he was resolute in his support for the group. “Boots is an important business,” Pessina said. “It will be for many years to come.”

data.title: Hedge funds bet against Scottish turbine company as Trump attacks ‘windmills’
data.description:
A major North Sea energy supplier has become the most shorted company on the UK stock market amid attacks from Donald Trump on offshore wind turbines.

Ashtead Technology, which rents equipment to offshore oil, gas and wind projects, has 7.5pc of its shares in the hands of hedge funds, which have sold them short to profit from an expected share price decline.

Hedge funds short companies when they think their share price will drop, selling shares and then buying them back at a lower price to profit from the drop in value.

According to data from the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA), funds including Ken Griffin’s Citadel, Acadian Asset Management and GLG Partners have shorted Ashtead as they bet on a further share price swoon in future.

Shares have already fallen 39pc this year amid bleak prospects for the North Sea.

The bets against the group have also escalated in recent days, with Acadian increasing its short position in the group.

It coincided with Mr Trump singling out the UK’s net zero policy, saying wind turbines were ruining the landscape and criticising high taxes on North Sea oil.

“I want to stop seeing them ruining that beautiful Scottish and English countryside with windmills and massive solar panels that go seven miles by seven miles, taking away farmland,” the US president told the UN on Tuesday.

Mr Trump described North Sea oil as “so highly taxed that no developer, no oil company can go there”.

“They’ve given up their powerful edge, a lot of the countries that we’re talking about, in oil and gas, such as essentially closing the great North Sea oil,” he said.

Victoria McCulloch, an analyst at RBC Capital, said a dearth of North Sea energy services companies for short-sellers to focus on was part of the reason for the high short interest in Ashtead.

She said the impact of Mr Trump’s tariffs and decreasing oil prices had forced Ashtead to cut revenue guidance earlier this year.

Net zero targets and windfall taxes have hammered offshore oil and gas activity in the North Sea, with projects stalling in recent years as successive governments prioritise net zero over oil and gas.

Last month, Jim Johnson, the chief executive of oil group Hunting, said he had been forced to close four of the company’s five UK centres including its main Scottish site because the Government had destroyed the North Sea offshore oil industry.

He said the closures were directly linked to government windfall taxes and over-zealous regulations imposed on the industry to reach net zero.

A spokesman from Ashtead Technology said: “As we look to move up to the Main Market of the London Stock Exchange, expected on Oct 6, we are wholly focused on executing against our proven growth strategy and assisting our customers with their critical subsea operations, with the support of our long-term shareholders.”

Citadel and Acadian were contacted for comment.
